Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd sit down with my laptop, put on the lecture recordings, and listen while doing other things around my apartment. I spent about 21 hours a week technically studying, but a lot of that time I wasn't fully present. I'd complete what needed to be done, but I was just going through the motions most of the time.
Q2: My pattern was surprisingly regular actually, but not because I was motivated. It was more like autopilot—I'd log in, do what was required, check things off. The consistency came from habit rather than any real drive to learn. Deadlines didn't stress me out much because I always submitted things on time, but that doesn't mean I was actually engaged with the material.
Q3: Usually it was just knowing I had to complete something or check if anything new was posted. I logged in pretty frequently—almost daily—but it was mechanical. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I wasn't really absorbing anything. It became more of a checkbox activity than genuine learning.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became routine pretty quickly. I'd wake up, make coffee, open the LMS while eating breakfast. It was habitual but hollow, if that makes sense. The routine was there, but the engagement wasn't. I could navigate the platform with my eyes closed, but that didn't mean I cared about what I was looking at.
Q5: The audio lectures and video content were most useful because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen while doing other things, which fit my lifestyle. But honestly, even saying they were 'useful' feels generous—I consumed a lot of content without really processing it deeply. The videos were long, and I watched many hours of them, but retention was another story.
Q6: The discussion boards were basically dead to me. I posted twice the entire course because it felt performative and pointless. I also ignored most of the supplementary readings—I'd skim them at best. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment grade, I couldn't find the motivation to care about it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly contributed to my grade and had clear instructions. I'm 27 and I've been doing this long enough to know what's busywork and what actually matters for passing. If I couldn't see the immediate payoff, I just couldn't bring myself to invest energy in it.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones I'd do because I had to, but optional ones? I couldn't generate the motivation. There's a big difference between 'this would be enriching' and 'you need this to pass.' I was operating in survival mode, not growth mode.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download the lecture audio and listen on walks or while cooking. I had notes in a separate notebook that I'd review sometimes. A lot of my studying happened away from the screen because I find it exhausting to be online constantly. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that.
Q10: There were many times I'd listen to a downloaded lecture multiple times while doing chores, trying to get concepts to stick. Or I'd sit with my notebook working through problems without being logged in anywhere. The system only sees clicks and timestamps, not the actual mental effort happening offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a video, access it once on the LMS, and then work with it offline for days. It was more convenient and less draining than constantly being connected. One download might represent hours of actual study time that never showed up in the logs.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt impossible. The stress was building, my motivation was already low, and I just couldn't make myself care. I kept showing up and completing assignments, but I was running on empty. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped my mood afterward, but they couldn't fix my fundamental disconnection from the material.
Q13: Fatigue was a huge factor. I was tired all the time, and juggling eight online courses was overwhelming even with good resources available. I'd log in, do the bare minimum, and log out. Confusion sometimes stopped me from engaging deeper—if I didn't understand something, I'd just move on rather than seek help.
Q14: Constantly. I'd have a video playing while scrolling my phone or staring out the window. The LMS would register that I watched 230+ minutes of video, but my brain was elsewhere for a lot of it. There's a difference between having something on and actually learning from it.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation repeatedly, even knowing it was crucial. The assignments I could handle—they were structured and manageable. But studying for exams required self-direction and deeper engagement that I just couldn't muster. I knew I was sabotaging myself, but I felt paralyzed.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing keeping me functional. I'd complete everything on time because the deadline gave me external structure. Without that pressure, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. But meeting deadlines didn't mean I was learning—it meant I was surviving.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior. I'd get good marks on assignments, which probably made me complacent. The positive feedback didn't motivate me to try harder on exams because I thought I understood the material better than I did. It was a false sense of security.
Q18: The course structure was clear enough, but it didn't inspire engagement. Everything was laid out, I knew what to do, but knowing and caring are different things. The structure made it easy to complete tasks without actually connecting with the content. It enabled my drifting, in a way.
Q19: The heavy reliance on video content suited my learning style but also made it easy to be passive. There wasn't much that forced active engagement. Discussion requirements were minimal, which I appreciated at the time but probably hurt me. The course made it too easy to coast.
Q20: I think that describes me perfectly. High activity can mean you're present but not engaged. You're clicking, watching, downloading—doing all the visible things—but your mind isn't in it. The LMS captures behavior, not comprehension or investment. Someone can be very active and still be completely checked out.
Q21: That's the opposite of me, but I understand it. Some people are efficient—they engage deeply in short bursts and don't need constant LMS presence. They might study effectively offline or just process information faster. Low activity doesn't mean low learning; it might mean focused, intentional engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long you stay. But they can't show whether you're actually thinking, whether the material is making sense, or whether you care at all. They capture the skeleton of engagement without the soul. My logs looked fine; my exam score tells a different story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it. It's not just completing tasks—it's wanting to understand, being curious, feeling like the material matters. I haven't felt that way in a long time. I was present but not engaged, active but not invested.
Q24: The data wouldn't show that I was going through a period of low motivation and questioning why I'm even doing this anymore. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness exercises that improved my mood but not my academic performance. It wouldn't show that I'm 27, burned out, and just trying to get through this. The numbers look okay on paper, but I failed the exam because completing tasks isn't the same as learning.
